title: Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome in 47-Year-Old Man with Severe Alcohol Use Disorder (12+ Beers/Day × 10 Years) Admitted After Motor Vehicle Accident: CIWA-Ar Symptom-Triggered Benzodiazepine Protocol, Thiamine Administration BEFORE Glucose to Prevent Wernicke Encephalopathy, Delirium Tremens Prevention + Seizure Prophylaxis, and Multidisciplinary Substance Use Disorder Treatment with MAT Referral
subtitle: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME (AWS) develops in chronic heavy drinkers within 6-48 hr of last drink + can progress to LIFE-THREATENING delirium tremens (DTs) without appropriate treatment; STAGES + TIMELINE: (1) STAGE 1 MILD (6-12 hr post last drink) = anxiety + tremor + insomnia + GI upset + mild autonomic (HR + BP); (2) STAGE 2 MODERATE (12-48 hr) = worsening tremor + diaphoresis + N/V + HTN + tachycardia + auditory/tactile/visual hallucinations (without confusion = ALCOHOLIC HALLUCINOSIS); (3) STAGE 3 SEIZURES (12-48 hr typically) = generalized tonic-clonic seizures (single or multiple); (4) STAGE 4 DELIRIUM TREMENS (DTs) (48-96 hr peak; can occur up to 7-10 days) = SEVERE autonomic instability + altered mental status + agitation + hallucinations + fever + seizures + 5-15% MORTALITY if untreated + 1-5% with treatment; RISK FACTORS for severe withdrawal/DTs = chronic heavy use + age >40 + comorbidities + prior history of DTs (single most predictive) + prior seizures + medical illness + concurrent infection + abnormal liver/electrolytes + thrombocytopenia + dehydration + heavy use within past 30 days; CIWA-Ar SCALE (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol — Revised) 10-item scale 0-67 measuring withdrawal severity for SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED therapy: nausea + tremor + autonomic + anxiety + agitation + tactile + auditory + visual hallucinations + headache + orientation; SCORES: <10 mild + 10-15 moderate + >15 moderate-severe + >20 severe + DT risk; ASSESS q1h initially when treatment started + q2-4h based on stability; SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED BENZODIAZEPINE protocol with CIWA-Ar threshold (e.g., LORAZEPAM 2-4 mg IV q1h PRN for CIWA ≥10) — more effective + lower total dose + shorter LOS than fixed-schedule; alternative FIXED-SCHEDULE for high-risk patients or institutions lacking CIWA monitoring capability; BENZODIAZEPINES = first-line + GABAA receptor positive allosteric modulators replace alcohol's effect + prevent seizures + reduce DT severity; choice = LORAZEPAM (Ativan; preferred in elderly + liver disease — no active metabolites + short-intermediate half-life), DIAZEPAM (Valium; long-acting metabolites + smooth withdrawal but accumulate in liver disease + elderly), CHLORDIAZEPOXIDE (Librium; long-acting + sometimes preferred for milder withdrawal); ROUTES = IV for unstable + ICU patients + PO for stable; FRONT-LOAD dosing for severe withdrawal/imminent DTs; PROPHYLACTIC PHENOBARBITAL or KETAMINE for refractory DTs (PICU-level + intubation often required); AVOID prolonged benzodiazepine use beyond acute withdrawal (dependence + accumulation); THIAMINE 100 mg IV BEFORE GLUCOSE administration is CRITICAL — IV glucose can precipitate WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY (acute thiamine deficiency triad = ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + confusion) + KORSAKOFF AMNESTIC SYNDROME (irreversible memory + confabulation if untreated) in thiamine-deficient chronic drinkers; THIAMINE 100 mg IV daily × 5 days then PO 100 mg daily long-term; FOLATE 1 mg + multivitamin + magnesium 2-4 g IV if low + electrolyte management; nutrition + hydration; HEALTHY DECISIONS = honest non-judgmental engagement + motivational interviewing + RISK STRATIFICATION + safe environment + 1:1 sitter for severe + family + chaplain + social worker; multidisciplinary including ADDICTION MEDICINE consultation + MEDICATION-ASSISTED TREATMENT (MAT) referral for outpatient (NALTREXONE oral or IM depot 380 mg q month; ACAMPROSATE 666 mg TID PO; DISULFIRAM 250 mg daily PO — produces aversion reaction with alcohol; GABAPENTIN off-label 300-900 mg TID; topiramate); 12-step program + AA + community support + relapse prevention + Naltrexone + Vivitrol injection + outpatient counseling + therapy + sober living + harm reduction approach when needed

labs.n: PRESENTATION + MVA + chronic heavy alcohol use disorder history
labs.v: 47 y/o male T. Sullivan presenting after MVA at 0700 today (single-vehicle accident — patient lost control of vehicle + hit tree at low speed; airbag deployed; seat belt in use; minor injuries including right forearm laceration 4 cm + bilateral arm bruising + neck strain; no LOC + no major injuries; cleared from CT head + CT C-spine; trauma + spine cleared); admitted to medicine for observation + alcohol withdrawal risk given chronic heavy alcohol use disorder + recent drink (~2 hr before MVA). PMH: alcohol use disorder × 10 years (12+ beers/day baseline + occasional liquor especially evenings + weekends; episodic binge use; CAGE 4/4 positive; AUDIT 25 = severe alcohol use; multiple prior outpatient detox attempts failed + 2 prior inpatient detox + 1 prior DTs episode 3 yr ago with seizures + ICU admission); HTN (untreated; rarely sees PCP); chronic pain (low back); occasional tobacco use; remote IV drug use × 5 yr ago (now clean); HCV status unknown — likely positive given prior IDU + chronic alcohol use + last screened 8 yr ago; PCP > 5 yr ago. SOCIAL: married 18 years to wife (45, RN at outside hospital — recognizes alcohol use disorder pattern); 2 teen daughters (15 + 13); recent JOB LOSS 6 months ago (construction supervisor — laid off; financial stress); social isolation increasing; family conflict around alcohol; wife considering separation; financial strain. PRECIPITANT for today's MVA = patient drove home from buying beer at 0700 + still impaired from overnight drinking + lost control on residential road. LAST DRINK approximately 6 AM today before MVA (~9.5 hr before medicine admission at 1530). CHRONIC heavy use pattern + dependence + tolerance established (12+ beers/day × 10 years). FAMILY HISTORY of alcohol use disorder (father + paternal grandfather both with severe AUD + cirrhosis + early death; family pattern); maternal grandmother with depression. CURRENT MEDICATIONS: none currently (no prescription medications); doesn't see PCP regularly; tried multivitamin previously. SOCIAL: wife concerned + at bedside + supportive but exhausted; daughters present (chaperoned by maternal aunt visiting); brother flying in from Boston (additional family support). ALLERGIES: PCN (mild rash 15 yr ago — likely outgrown). LAST DETOX: 3 years ago (inpatient × 5 days + DTs episode + seizures + ICU; discharged with AA referral but relapsed within 1 month). MAINTENANCE ATTEMPTS: tried Antabuse briefly in past (didn't tolerate); never tried Naltrexone or Acamprosate or Vivitrol injection or 3HP rifapentine/INH for LTBI (TB exposure history? checked); not on MAT currently. RECOGNIZED RISK FACTORS for severe withdrawal/DTs in this patient: chronic heavy use × 10 yr (heavy + long duration); age 47 (>40); prior history of DTs with seizures (single most predictive — strong risk); recent heavy use within past 30 days (continuous); concurrent medical illness/MVA + stress + dehydration. ED labs sent: CBC + BMP + LFT + lipase + GGT + amylase + glucose + magnesium + phosphate + folate + B12 + alcohol level + drug screen + ABG + UA; HCV screen + HIV screen. ETHANOL LEVEL at ED 0830 = 0.18 g/dL (180 mg/dL — patient acknowledged 6 AM drinking; legal limit 0.08; level consistent with chronic heavy use + still elevated 1.5 hr post-last drink); CBC = Hgb 14 + WBC 8.5 + platelets 165 (mild thrombocytopenia — alcohol-related); BMP normal + Mg 1.5 (LOW — common in chronic alcohol use + relevant to seizure threshold + treatment); Phos 2.2 (low); folate pending (likely low); B12 pending; LFTs = AST 145 + ALT 78 + AST:ALT ratio >2 typical alcohol-related liver disease; alk phos 110; T bili 1.4; GGT 305 (markedly elevated; specific for alcohol-related liver disease); INR 1.4 (slight elevation; alcohol-related coagulopathy); albumin 3.5 (mild decline; alcohol-related); HCV antibody POSITIVE (chronic HCV likely; viral load pending; recall remote IV drug use); HIV negative; UA mild ketones + protein; ABG normal pH + slight respiratory alkalosis from anxiety + early withdrawal.
labs.r: ALCOHOL USE DISORDER + chronic heavy use + multiple risk factors for severe withdrawal/DTs = recognition + planning essential. Risk factor stratification critical. RN role: history-taking + lab interpretation + escalation + family support + multidisciplinary engagement.

labs.n: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL STAGES + CIWA-Ar SCALE + symptom-triggered protocol
labs.v: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL SYNDROME (AWS) STAGES + TIMELINE per ASAM (American Society of Addiction Medicine) + clinical practice guidelines: (1) STAGE 1 MILD (6-12 hr post last drink) = anxiety + tremor + insomnia + GI upset (nausea + decreased appetite) + mild autonomic (HR + BP elevation); typically resolves with appropriate treatment + does not progress in 80% of patients; (2) STAGE 2 MODERATE (12-48 hr) = worsening tremor + diaphoresis + N/V + HTN + tachycardia + AUDITORY/TACTILE/VISUAL HALLUCINATIONS without confusion = ALCOHOLIC HALLUCINOSIS (vs DTs which has CONFUSION); some patients progress to DTs; (3) STAGE 3 SEIZURES (12-48 hr typically; can occur up to 6 days) = GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC SEIZURES (single or multiple); ~10% of untreated severe withdrawal patients experience seizures; risk factors = prior alcohol seizures, prior DTs, abnormal magnesium, abnormal calcium, chronic heavy use, age >40; (4) STAGE 4 DELIRIUM TREMENS (DTs) (48-96 hr peak; can occur up to 7-10 days) = SEVERE autonomic instability (HR + BP + fever) + altered mental status + agitation + hallucinations + tremor + 5-15% MORTALITY IF UNTREATED + 1-5% MORTALITY WITH APPROPRIATE TREATMENT; PATHOPHYSIOLOGY = chronic alcohol → GABA receptor downregulation + glutamate (NMDA) receptor upregulation → acute alcohol cessation → relative GABA deficiency + glutamate excess = CNS hyperexcitability + autonomic instability + seizures + DTs. RISK FACTORS for SEVERE withdrawal/DTs: (1) PRIOR DTs OR alcohol-related seizures (SINGLE MOST PREDICTIVE — 50% recurrence); (2) chronic heavy use (years + amount); (3) age >40; (4) comorbidities (medical illness + infection + electrolyte abnormalities + hepatic disease + thrombocytopenia + dehydration); (5) heavy use within past 30 days (vs gradual cutdown); (6) concurrent benzodiazepine or opioid withdrawal. CIWA-Ar SCALE (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol — Revised) — STANDARD ASSESSMENT TOOL for withdrawal severity + symptom-triggered therapy; 10 ITEMS each scored 0-7 (orientation 0-4): (1) NAUSEA + VOMITING; (2) TREMOR (observation arms outstretched); (3) AUTONOMIC (paroxysmal sweats); (4) ANXIETY; (5) AGITATION; (6) TACTILE DISTURBANCES (pruritus + paresthesias + tactile hallucinations); (7) AUDITORY DISTURBANCES (hyperacusis + auditory hallucinations); (8) VISUAL DISTURBANCES (sensitivity + visual hallucinations); (9) HEADACHE + FULLNESS in head; (10) ORIENTATION + CLOUDING OF SENSORIUM (0 = oriented x3; up to 4 = disoriented to time + place + person + situation); TOTAL SCORE 0-67; CUTOFFS: <10 mild + 10-15 moderate + >15 moderate-severe + >20 severe + DT risk. ASSESSMENT FREQUENCY: q1h initially when treatment started; q2-4h when stable + improving; q1h again if worsening + after intervention. SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED BENZODIAZEPINE PROTOCOL (preferred over fixed-schedule for monitoring capability + ICU setting) = use CIWA-Ar threshold to administer benzodiazepine doses; EXAMPLE: CIWA ≥10 = LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV q1h PRN; CIWA ≥15 = lorazepam 4 mg IV q1h PRN; CIWA ≥20 = lorazepam 4 mg IV q30 min PRN + ICU consultation; CIWA ≥25 = continuous infusion or fixed-schedule + intubation if needed; reassess CIWA-Ar 30-60 min after each dose; advantages = lower total benzodiazepine dose + shorter ICU stay + lower respiratory depression; LIMITATIONS = requires trained staff + frequent assessment + may underdose if CIWA misapplied; FIXED-SCHEDULE BENZODIAZEPINE PROTOCOL = alternative for high-risk patients (prior DTs + severe withdrawal anticipated) or facilities without CIWA capability — example LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV q4h × 24 hr then taper; advantages = simpler + may prevent under-dosing in busy units; LIMITATIONS = higher cumulative dose + longer LOS in some studies; CHOICE varies by institution + patient factors.
labs.r: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL STAGES + CIWA-Ar SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED PROTOCOL = standard ASAM + clinical practice. RECOGNIZE risk factors + stage + assess CIWA-Ar + administer appropriate benzodiazepine. RN role: assessment + medication administration + monitoring + escalation.

labs.v: BENZODIAZEPINES = FIRST-LINE for alcohol withdrawal — GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulators replace alcohol's effect on GABA + prevent seizures + reduce DT severity + mortality benefit. CHOICE of benzodiazepine: (1) LORAZEPAM (Ativan) — PREFERRED in ELDERLY + LIVER DISEASE + ICU + nursing home; SHORTER intermediate half-life (10-20 hr) + glucuronide conjugation (NO active metabolites) + safer in liver impairment + less accumulation; IV onset 5-15 min + PO onset 30-60 min + IM onset 15-30 min; dose 2-4 mg IV q1-4h PRN per CIWA-Ar or scheduled; max often 24-48 mg/day; can give IM if no IV access; (2) DIAZEPAM (Valium) — LONG-ACTING + active metabolites (desmethyldiazepam + nordiazepam + 30-200 hr) → SMOOTH WITHDRAWAL TAPER (self-tapering pharmacologically) but ACCUMULATION in liver disease + elderly + prolonged sedation + respiratory depression risk; dose 5-10 mg IV q1-4h PRN; max often 100-200 mg/day; PO option; preferred for younger + healthier patients without liver disease; (3) CHLORDIAZEPOXIDE (Librium) — LONG-ACTING + active metabolites + smooth taper; PO only (not IV); preferred for OUTPATIENT withdrawal management + milder withdrawal; dose 50-100 mg q4-6h × 24 hr then taper; (4) MIDAZOLAM — IV only + SHORT-ACTING + rapid onset; preferred for INTUBATION + ICU + procedural sedation; CONTINUOUS INFUSION for severe withdrawal not responsive to bolus; (5) OXAZEPAM — shorter-acting + no active metabolites; PO only; useful in elderly + liver disease; less common in US. ROUTES = IV for unstable + ICU + severe withdrawal; PO for stable + mild-moderate; IM acceptable but slower onset + variable absorption. FRONT-LOADING for severe withdrawal/imminent DTs = larger initial dose to rapidly achieve therapeutic level + prevent progression; EXAMPLE: diazepam 10-20 mg IV q10-15 min until CIWA-Ar <10 or sedation; then maintenance doses. CONTINUOUS INFUSION for refractory severe withdrawal/DTs = midazolam 1-10 mg/hr titrated; lorazepam 1-3 mg/hr titrated; PROPOFOL or DEXMEDETOMIDINE adjunct in refractory cases (PICU/ICU level). PHENOBARBITAL (long-acting barbiturate) — alternative or adjunct to benzodiazepines for severe + refractory withdrawal; smoother taper + reliable + less respiratory depression than diazepam; dose 200-300 mg IV/IM load + 30-60 mg q4h PO taper; CAUTION respiratory depression + need close monitoring. KETAMINE — NMDA antagonist + adjunct for refractory severe withdrawal/DTs in ICU; 0.5-1 mg/kg IV bolus then 0.1-0.5 mg/kg/hr infusion; rapid effect + opioid-sparing + dissociative + monitoring. AVOID OUTPATIENT BENZODIAZEPINE prescription beyond acute withdrawal taper (dependence + ongoing use); transition to non-benzodiazepine maintenance options (gabapentin for ongoing anxiety + sleep + sometimes; SSRI/SNRI for depression/anxiety; Naltrexone for AUD; outpatient counseling). MONITORING: respiratory rate + sedation level + cardiac monitor + pulse ox; flumazenil reversal RESERVED for life-threatening respiratory depression (CAUTION — can precipitate seizures in benzodiazepine-dependent patient). DOSE CALCULATIONS for THIS PATIENT (47 y/o + chronic heavy use + prior DTs + high risk + recent seizure): aggressive front-loading with lorazepam 4-8 mg IV q15-60 min titrated to CIWA-Ar <10 + sedation; ICU level + continuous monitoring + close observation + ready to escalate to phenobarbital + ketamine + intubation if refractory.
labs.r: BENZODIAZEPINES first-line for alcohol withdrawal; CHOICE = lorazepam preferred in elderly/liver disease (no active metabolites); diazepam smooth taper but accumulation risk; chlordiazepoxide for outpatient mild; midazolam for ICU + continuous infusion; phenobarbital + ketamine for refractory; AVOID outpatient long-term. RN role: dose titration + monitoring + escalation.

labs.n: THIAMINE BEFORE GLUCOSE — Wernicke encephalopathy prevention
labs.v: WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY = ACUTE NEUROLOGIC EMERGENCY from THIAMINE (vitamin B1) DEFICIENCY in chronic alcohol users + malnutrition + hyperemesis + bariatric surgery + chronic GI disease + chronic kidney disease + chemotherapy + dialysis + magnesium deficiency. CLASSIC TRIAD (rare to see all three; many patients atypical presentation): (1) OPHTHALMOPLEGIA (nystagmus + lateral rectus palsy + ophthalmoparesis); (2) ATAXIA (broad-based stance + truncal ataxia + limb ataxia); (3) ENCEPHALOPATHY (confusion + altered mental status + apathy + memory disturbance). UNTREATED progresses to KORSAKOFF AMNESTIC SYNDROME (irreversible memory loss + confabulation + cognitive impairment) — 'WERNICKE-KORSAKOFF SYNDROME'; mortality 17-20% if untreated; significant morbidity in survivors. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY = thiamine is essential cofactor for KEY GLUCOSE METABOLISM ENZYMES (pyruvate dehydrogenase + α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase + transketolase); thiamine deficiency → impaired glucose metabolism + neuronal injury especially in mammillary bodies + thalamus + brainstem. CRITICAL PRINCIPLE = ADMINISTER THIAMINE BEFORE GLUCOSE in chronic alcohol users + suspected thiamine deficiency; IV GLUCOSE administration without thiamine can PRECIPITATE WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY by acutely depleting marginal thiamine stores (glucose metabolism requires thiamine; sudden glucose load depletes thiamine reserve in deficient patient → acute deficiency → Wernicke). DOSING: THIAMINE 100 mg IV (or IM if no IV access) BEFORE any IV glucose/dextrose administration in chronic heavy drinkers + malnourished patients; THIAMINE 100 mg IV daily × 5 days for acute withdrawal + nutritional support; THIAMINE 100 mg PO daily long-term outpatient. SOME PROTOCOLS recommend HIGHER DOSE = 500 mg IV TID × 2-3 days then 250 mg IV daily × 5 days for established Wernicke; high-dose IV thiamine for acute encephalopathy + chronic + severe deficiency. THIAMINE administration: IV PUSH SLOW over 5-10 min OR DILUTE in 50 mL NS or D5W run over 30 min; well-tolerated; ANAPHYLAXIS rare (more common with IM); RARELY contraindicated. CONCURRENT supplementation: FOLATE 1 mg PO/IV daily (folate deficiency common); MULTIVITAMIN daily; MAGNESIUM 2-4 g IV (magnesium deficiency common + worsens seizure threshold + thiamine metabolism); VITAMIN B12 1000 mcg daily; nutrition + protein + caloric support. THIS PATIENT: chronic heavy alcohol use 10 years + likely malnourished + acute illness + needs IV access for hydration + medications + treatment for withdrawal → THIAMINE 100 mg IV given BEFORE any glucose-containing fluid (D5W or D5NS); patient also received magnesium 2 g IV + folate 1 mg PO + multivitamin + B12 1000 mcg IM; concerns about Wernicke encephalopathy + Korsakoff syndrome addressed; outpatient continuation of thiamine + multivitamin + nutrition counseling + abstinence + ongoing supervision.
labs.r: THIAMINE BEFORE GLUCOSE is CRITICAL principle in chronic alcohol users + malnutrition to prevent Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. Thiamine 100 mg IV daily × 5 days + multivitamin + folate + magnesium + B12 + nutrition. RN role: medication administration + monitoring + family education.

labs.v: DELIRIUM TREMENS (DTs) = MOST SEVERE FORM of alcohol withdrawal; SEVERE autonomic instability + altered mental status + agitation + hallucinations + 5-15% MORTALITY IF UNTREATED + 1-5% MORTALITY WITH APPROPRIATE TREATMENT (early aggressive benzodiazepines + supportive care). TIMING: typically 48-96 hr post last drink (peak 72-96 hr); can occur up to 7-10 days; RARELY before 24 hr; less common with continuous monitoring + early benzo treatment. RECOGNITION: CIWA-Ar typically >20 + agitation + confusion + global disorientation (versus alcoholic hallucinosis where orientation preserved with hallucinations); SEVERE autonomic instability (HR >120 + SBP >160 + RR >24 + fever >38°C); HALLUCINATIONS (visual + tactile + auditory; classic 'lilliputian' formed visual hallucinations); GLOBAL DISORIENTATION + confusion + perception impairment; TREMOR severe; DIAPHORESIS profuse; AGITATION + restlessness; SEIZURES (50% of DT patients have prior seizures); DEHYDRATION + electrolyte derangement; sometimes psychosis + paranoia; LIFE-THREATENING. RISK STRATIFICATION + PREDICTION: PAWSS (Prediction of Alcohol Withdrawal Severity Scale) ≥4 predicts DTs/severe withdrawal; previously discussed risk factors. PREVENTION: (1) EARLY recognition of withdrawal risk + CIWA-Ar assessment + symptom-triggered or scheduled benzodiazepine protocol; (2) AGGRESSIVE benzodiazepine therapy for moderate-severe withdrawal; (3) CORRECT electrolyte abnormalities + magnesium + phosphate + dehydration; (4) THIAMINE before glucose + nutritional support; (5) CARDIAC MONITORING + continuous monitoring + close observation; (6) MULTIDISCIPLINARY consultation including addiction medicine; (7) 1:1 SITTER for severe withdrawal + agitation + safety; (8) ICU TRANSFER for severe withdrawal/imminent DTs (continuous monitoring + escalation of benzodiazepines + supportive care). TREATMENT of established DTs: (1) ICU LEVEL of CARE with continuous monitoring + close observation + 1:1 sitter + safe environment + restraint AVOIDANCE if possible (worsens agitation + can cause harm); (2) AGGRESSIVE BENZODIAZEPINE THERAPY — large doses needed (often 100-300+ mg lorazepam-equivalent in 24 hr) titrated to CIWA-Ar <10 + appropriate sedation; continuous infusion may be needed (midazolam 1-10 mg/hr titrated); (3) PHENOBARBITAL (300-600 mg IV/IM load + maintenance) as alternative or adjunct; (4) KETAMINE (NMDA antagonist) infusion 0.1-0.5 mg/kg/hr as refractory adjunct; (5) PROPOFOL or DEXMEDETOMIDINE infusion in ICU for refractory; (6) INTUBATION if respiratory failure or excessive sedation; (7) SUPPORTIVE CARE — IV fluids + electrolyte replacement + nutrition + cardiac monitoring + vital signs q1h + neuro checks; (8) treat concurrent issues (infection + electrolyte + dehydration + nutrition + medical illness); (9) AVOID benzodiazepine reversal with flumazenil (can precipitate seizures + worse withdrawal); (10) MULTIDISCIPLINARY + addiction medicine + psychiatry + family + chaplain + social work + emotional support throughout + post-event debriefing for staff; (11) MOST cases recover with supportive care + 1-5 days of DT phase + resolution; SOME progress + require longer treatment + multidisciplinary management; (12) DEATH from DTs = arrhythmia + cardiovascular collapse + respiratory failure + seizure + hyperthermia + electrolyte derangement + aspiration + comorbid illness.
labs.r: DTs = severe alcohol withdrawal with significant mortality even with treatment; PREVENTION through early recognition + benzodiazepine therapy + nutrition + multidisciplinary; TREATMENT = ICU + aggressive benzodiazepine + phenobarbital/ketamine adjunct + supportive care; AVOID flumazenil. RN role: recognition + escalation + medication + monitoring + family + multidisciplinary.

labs.v: MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE for alcohol use disorder + withdrawal: (1) ADDICTION MEDICINE specialist or board-certified addiction medicine physician — diagnosis + management + MAT + outpatient referral + chronic disease perspective; (2) ADDICTION COUNSELOR + licensed alcohol/drug counselor (LADC) — therapy + counseling + motivational interviewing + relapse prevention + community resources; (3) SOCIAL WORKER — family + insurance + community + employment + housing + legal + relationship support; (4) CHAPLAIN + spiritual care + meaning-making + AA/12-step spiritual framework + family rituals; (5) NUTRITIONIST/DIETITIAN — nutrition counseling + supplements + meal planning + healthy lifestyle; (6) PHYSICAL THERAPIST + occupational therapy if functional limitations; (7) MENTAL HEALTH (psychiatry + psychology) — co-occurring conditions (depression + anxiety + PTSD + bipolar) common in AUD + warrants treatment; (8) FAMILY THERAPIST + couples therapy + family counseling — family system affected by AUD; (9) PEER SUPPORT + AA + SMART Recovery + Refuge Recovery + other recovery communities + sponsor relationship; (10) PCP medical home for chronic disease management including HCV + hypertension + chronic pain + future preventive care; (11) DENTIST for oral health; (12) EAP + workplace accommodations + return-to-work planning; (13) LEGAL if relevant (DUI + custody + workplace). MEDICATION-ASSISTED TREATMENT (MAT) for alcohol use disorder per ASAM + clinical guidelines: (1) NALTREXONE — opioid receptor antagonist; reduces alcohol craving + reward; ORAL 50 mg daily OR IM DEPOT (Vivitrol) 380 mg q month; PREFERRED for high relapse risk + adherence challenges (depot ensures 4 weeks of coverage); side effects = nausea + headache + insomnia + injection site reaction (depot); CONTRAINDICATIONS = current opioid use or imminent (precipitates withdrawal); hepatitis (warning) — careful + monitor LFTs; pregnancy use with caution; (2) ACAMPROSATE (Campral) — modulates glutamate + GABA; 666 mg PO TID; reduces relapse risk; safer in liver impairment than naltrexone (renal clearance instead); side effects = GI + headache; needs adherence × 3-4 doses daily challenging; (3) DISULFIRAM (Antabuse) — inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase → if patient drinks → severe AVERSION REACTION (flushing + headache + N/V + chest pain + palpitations + can be severe); 250 mg PO daily; requires patient + family support + commitment + monitoring + only for motivated patients; CAUTIONS = liver disease + cardiac + neuropathy + pregnancy; older agent + less commonly used now; (4) GABAPENTIN — off-label for alcohol use disorder + reduces craving + anxiety + neuropathy; 300-900 mg TID; safer profile; alternative when other agents not tolerated; (5) TOPIRAMATE — off-label for alcohol use disorder + reduces craving; 50-300 mg daily; side effects = cognitive + weight loss + nephrolithiasis; alternative. AA + 12-STEP PROGRAMS = peer support + spiritual framework + sponsor + meetings (in-person + online); SMART Recovery (cognitive-behavioral approach) + LifeRing + Women for Sobriety + Refuge Recovery + faith-based programs alternatives; choice based on patient preferences + values + cultural fit. RECOVERY HOUSING + sober living + transitional housing for vulnerable patients. RELAPSE PREVENTION strategies — identify triggers + coping skills + alternative behaviors + social support + reward system + handling slips + recommit + maintain connection with provider + counselor + community + medication. OUTPATIENT INTENSIVE TREATMENT = IOP (intensive outpatient program) + PHP (partial hospitalization program) + residential treatment + recovery support. HCV management — chronic HCV likely + screening + treatment with direct-acting antivirals (DAAs); cures >95% + 12-week course; PCP or hepatology + abstinence supports treatment success. LONG-TERM follow-up + comprehensive integrated care + family + community + multidisciplinary + chronic disease perspective.
labs.r: MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE + MAT + outpatient + recovery framework. AUD is chronic disease + relapse common (5-7 attempts typical). MAT improves outcomes + appropriate selection + monitoring + adherence. AA + community + family + chronic disease management approach. RN role: education + advocacy + community + lifelong support.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: NO active suicidal ideation or homicidal ideation (alcohol intoxication + withdrawal sometimes associated with worsening mood + safety risk; routinely screened); NO acute cardiac events (no MI + no arrhythmia + no acute heart failure currently); NO acute pulmonary embolism or stroke; NO sepsis (cultures pending if concerns); NO acute hepatic failure (LFTs abnormal but stable; INR mildly elevated; albumin mild decline + not in fulminant failure); NO acute GI bleeding (no melena + no hematemesis + no PUD acutely); NO acute pancreatitis (lipase normal); NO acute renal injury (Cr normal for age); NO acute mental health crisis beyond withdrawal (no acute psychosis + no acute mania + no acute severe depression); NO unstable cardiovascular issues acutely; NO acute traumatic brain injury (CT head negative); NO concurrent illegal substance use (urine drug screen negative for opioids/cocaine/amphetamines/cannabis/benzodiazepines); NO active legal issues (DUI charges may be pending from MVA — patient + family considering legal counsel + insurance); insurance + multidisciplinary team available; family supportive (wife + daughters + brother); chaplain + social worker engaged. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: PATIENT MOTIVATED + receptive + grateful + recognizing the wake-up call from MVA + family conflict + impact on daughters; WIFE supportive (RN with medical literacy + has been concerned + relieved that treatment is starting); BROTHER flying in to provide additional support; CHAPLAIN + religious + spiritual support (family identifies as Catholic + chaplain coordinated); SOCIAL WORKER + addiction medicine + comprehensive multidisciplinary support; INSURANCE covers MAT + Vivitrol injection + outpatient counseling + therapy; OUTPATIENT MAT readily available + Naltrexone IM monthly + ongoing engagement; AA + 12-step + community resources + church support; INTACT family system + employment history (will need to address job loss + return-to-work planning); HCV potential cure with direct-acting antivirals (12-week course + >95% cure); RAPID RECOGNITION + appropriate treatment initiated within hours of admission + before progression to severe DTs (though seizure occurred); FAMILY meeting + transparent + compassionate engagement + receptive throughout; psychiatric stable + no co-occurring acute mental health crisis; no concurrent illegal substance use; cognitive intact + capacity for decision-making + receptive to recovery; multidisciplinary team experienced + culturally sensitive.
labs.r: Despite serious presentation + chronic AUD + prior DTs + seizure during admission, multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS support recovery: motivation + family + multidisciplinary + insurance + community + MAT + AA + religious/spiritual + cognitive intact + receptive + rapid treatment initiation. Standard expected positive trajectory with comprehensive intervention + lifelong AUD management.

ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL MANAGEMENT per ASAM-aligned institutional protocol:

SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED BENZODIAZEPINE PROTOCOL: LORAZEPAM 2-4 mg IV q1h PRN for CIWA-Ar ≥10; escalate to 4 mg IV q30 min for CIWA ≥20; continuous infusion for refractory severe withdrawal (midazolam 1-10 mg/hr titrated)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this alcohol withdrawal admission?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically + alcohol withdrawal significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: ALCOHOL USE DISORDER SEVERE × 10 years (12+ beers/day baseline + occasional liquor; CAGE 4/4 positive; AUDIT 25; 2 prior inpatient detox + 1 prior DTs with seizures + ICU 3 yr ago); LAST DRINK 6 AM today (~9.5 hr before admission) = entering STAGE 1 withdrawal; multiple risk factors for severe withdrawal/DTs (prior DTs MOST PREDICTIVE + chronic heavy use + age >40 + recent heavy use + medical stress from MVA)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severe AUD + multiple risk factors for severe withdrawal/DTs. Foundation for assessment + planning + risk stratification. RN role: history + risk factor identification + anticipation.

opts.text: CIWA-Ar SCALE assessment q1h initially + symptom-triggered benzodiazepine protocol; initial CIWA-Ar = 12 (MODERATE — triggers benzo therapy); escalating to 21 (SEVERE; DT risk) by 14 hr post last drink → withdrawal seizure × 1 at 18 hr post last drink → aggressive ICU management with continuous monitoring + 1:1 sitter + escalated benzo protocol
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — CIWA-Ar assessment + symptom-triggered protocol + escalation. Standard ASAM practice. RN role: assessment + medication administration + monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: BENZODIAZEPINES FIRST-LINE — LORAZEPAM (Ativan) preferred in this patient (no active metabolites + safer in liver + ICU); 2-4 mg IV q1h PRN per CIWA-Ar; escalation to 4 mg IV q30 min for CIWA ≥20; continuous infusion (midazolam 1-10 mg/hr) or PHENOBARBITAL or KETAMINE for refractory severe DTs; AVOID flumazenil (can precipitate seizures); AVOID outpatient long-term benzodiazepine prescription
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — benzodiazepine pharmacology + protocol. Standard practice. RN role: medication administration + monitoring + escalation + family education.

opts.text: THIAMINE 100 mg IV given BEFORE any GLUCOSE administration — CRITICAL principle to prevent WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY (ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + encephalopathy triad) + KORSAKOFF AMNESTIC SYNDROME (irreversible memory loss + confabulation) in chronic alcohol users with marginal thiamine stores; thiamine essential cofactor for glucose metabolism; sudden glucose load depletes thiamine reserve + precipitates Wernicke; 100 mg IV daily × 5 days then PO 100 mg daily long-term + folate + multivitamin + magnesium + B12
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Wernicke-Korsakoff prevention. THIAMINE BEFORE GLUCOSE is critical principle in chronic alcohol users. Standard practice. RN role: medication administration + family education + nutrition.

opts.text: WITHDRAWAL SEIZURE at 18 hr post last drink (~ Stage 3 timing 12-48 hr); generalized tonic-clonic × 90 sec; LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV given during/after seizure; seizure precautions + bed rail padded + suction + airway; ICU transfer activated for continuous monitoring + 1:1 sitter + escalated benzo protocol; risk for further seizures + progression to DTs
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — withdrawal seizure recognition + immediate response + ICU transfer + escalation. Standard ASAM practice. RN role: recognition + safety + medication + escalation + family communication.

opts.text: DELIRIUM TREMENS (DTs) RISK + prevention + recognition — typically 48-96 hr post last drink (peak 72-96 hr); SEVERE autonomic instability + altered mental status + agitation + hallucinations + 5-15% mortality if untreated + 1-5% with treatment; prevention via early benzodiazepine + supportive care + multidisciplinary + ICU level care; AVOID restraints (worsens agitation + can cause harm); AGGRESSIVE benzodiazepine therapy (often 100-300+ mg lorazepam equivalent in 24 hr)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — DT recognition + prevention + treatment. Severe AWS complication. RN role: recognition + escalation + medication + safety + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE + MAT outpatient: addiction medicine + addiction counselor + social worker + chaplain + nutritionist + family therapist + PCP + hepatology (HCV management); NALTREXONE IM DEPOT (Vivitrol) 380 mg q month preferred for this patient; AA + 12-step + community + sober living; family therapy + couples; financial + employment; HCV direct-acting antiviral treatment + cure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — comprehensive multidisciplinary care + MAT + lifelong AUD management. Standard practice. RN role: coordination + education + advocacy + family + community + insurance.

opts.text: FAMILY-CENTERED + multidisciplinary support: wife (RN at outside hospital + supportive but exhausted) + 2 teen daughters + brother flying in + chaplain + social worker; transparent + compassionate communication + emotional support + family therapy + couples therapy + cultural sensitivity (family Catholic + chaplain coordinated); patient receptive + motivated + grateful + recognizing wake-up call
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family-centered + emotional support + multidisciplinary engagement. Foundation for sustained recovery. RN role: facilitate + advocate + emotional support.

opts.text: Allergies: PCN (mild rash 15 yr ago) — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. No medication contraindications in current regimen. Document and proceed. Not the priority cue but important for safety.

opts.text: Administer SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg IV bolus to alkalinize blood + treat lactic acidosis + improve withdrawal symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. NO INDICATION for bicarbonate in standard alcohol withdrawal. Bicarbonate would be considered ONLY for severe metabolic acidosis with hemodynamic compromise or pH <6.9. Standard treatment = benzodiazepines + thiamine + electrolyte replacement + hydration + supportive care. Bicarbonate not indicated.

opts.text: Administer GLUCOSE 50% IV bolus immediately to provide energy + correct hypoglycemia + improve mental status; alcohol withdrawal patients are typically dehydrated + need rapid energy
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS approach. GLUCOSE WITHOUT THIAMINE FIRST can precipitate WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY in chronic alcohol users with marginal thiamine. CRITICAL PRINCIPLE = THIAMINE 100 mg IV BEFORE any glucose-containing fluid; thiamine is essential cofactor for glucose metabolism + sudden glucose load depletes thiamine reserve. Standard: thiamine first + then glucose if needed for hypoglycemia (verify hypoglycemia present + use D50 if needed; typically not needed in this patient who is alert + oriented + appropriate).

opts.text: Apply 4-point physical restraints immediately to prevent patient from harming self or others during alcohol withdrawal; restraints will keep patient safe + control agitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. RESTRAINTS during alcohol withdrawal WORSEN agitation + can cause physical harm + violate dignity + may not be necessary if appropriate medication + 1:1 sitter + safe environment. STANDARD = 1:1 sitter for severe withdrawal/agitation + safe environment + bed rail padded + remove dangerous items + appropriate benzodiazepine therapy to control symptoms + multidisciplinary support. Restraints reserved for truly emergent situations with extensive documentation + alternatives tried + time-limited per institutional + state regulations.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What alcohol withdrawal + pharm + mental health principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL STAGES + TIMELINE per ASAM: STAGE 1 mild (6-12 hr post last drink — anxiety + tremor + insomnia + autonomic); STAGE 2 moderate (12-48 hr — worsening + hallucinations without confusion = alcoholic hallucinosis); STAGE 3 seizures (12-48 hr — generalized tonic-clonic); STAGE 4 DTs (48-96 hr peak — severe autonomic + altered mental status + agitation + hallucinations + confusion + 5-15% mortality if untreated)
opts.rat: CORRECT — alcohol withdrawal stages + timeline framework. Standard knowledge. Foundation for recognition + management + anticipation.

opts.text: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY = chronic alcohol → GABA-A receptor DOWNREGULATION + GLUTAMATE (NMDA) receptor UPREGULATION → acute alcohol cessation → relative GABA deficiency + glutamate excess = CNS HYPEREXCITABILITY + autonomic instability + seizures + DTs; BENZODIAZEPINES first-line because positive allosteric modulators of GABA-A receptors REPLACE alcohol's effect + suppress hyperexcitability + prevent seizures + reduce DT severity + mortality benefit
opts.rat: CORRECT — pathophysiology + treatment rationale framework. Standard knowledge. Foundation for understanding + treatment.

opts.text: RISK FACTORS for SEVERE WITHDRAWAL/DTs: prior DTs OR alcohol-related seizures (SINGLE MOST PREDICTIVE — 50% recurrence); chronic heavy use (years + amount); age >40; comorbidities (medical illness + infection + electrolyte + hepatic disease + thrombocytopenia + dehydration); heavy use within past 30 days; concurrent benzodiazepine or opioid withdrawal; CIWA-Ar SCALE 10-item 0-67 measuring severity for symptom-triggered therapy
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk factors + CIWA-Ar framework. Standard practice. Foundation for risk stratification + monitoring.

opts.text: BENZODIAZEPINES first-line for alcohol withdrawal: LORAZEPAM preferred in elderly + liver disease + ICU (no active metabolites + safer); DIAZEPAM long-acting + active metabolites + smooth taper but accumulation in liver disease; CHLORDIAZEPOXIDE PO outpatient; MIDAZOLAM IV continuous infusion ICU; symptom-triggered (CIWA-Ar threshold) preferred over fixed-schedule when monitoring capable + reduces total dose + LOS; FIXED-schedule alternative for high-risk patients or institutions lacking CIWA capability
opts.rat: CORRECT — benzodiazepine choice + dosing + protocol framework. Standard ASAM practice. Foundation for medication selection + administration.

opts.text: THIAMINE BEFORE GLUCOSE is CRITICAL principle in chronic alcohol users + malnutrition + suspected thiamine deficiency to PREVENT WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY (acute thiamine deficiency triad = ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + encephalopathy) + KORSAKOFF AMNESTIC SYNDROME (irreversible memory loss + confabulation if untreated); thiamine 100 mg IV daily × 5 days + folate 1 mg + multivitamin + magnesium + B12 + nutrition support; high-dose 500 mg IV TID × 2-3 days for established Wernicke
opts.rat: CORRECT — Wernicke-Korsakoff prevention framework. Standard practice. RN role: medication administration + family education.

opts.text: DT TREATMENT in ICU = aggressive benzodiazepine therapy (large doses; often 100-300+ mg lorazepam equivalent in 24 hr) + phenobarbital (200-300 mg load) + ketamine infusion + propofol + dexmedetomidine + intubation if needed + 1:1 sitter + safe environment + AVOID restraints if possible + supportive care + multidisciplinary; mortality 1-5% with appropriate treatment + 5-15% if untreated
opts.rat: CORRECT — DT treatment framework. ICU level + aggressive benzodiazepine + multidisciplinary. Standard practice.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY + MAT + outpatient = addiction medicine + addiction counselor + social worker + chaplain + nutritionist + family therapist + PCP + hepatology (HCV management); MAT options = NALTREXONE oral or IM DEPOT (Vivitrol) 380 mg q month + ACAMPROSATE 666 mg TID + DISULFIRAM 250 mg + GABAPENTIN off-label + topiramate; AA + 12-step + SMART Recovery + community + sober living; chronic disease perspective + 5-7 relapse attempts typical + lifelong management
opts.rat: CORRECT — multidisciplinary + MAT + outpatient framework. Standard ASAM practice. Foundation for lifelong AUD management.

opts.text: Administer SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg IV bolus to alkalinize blood + treat lactic acidosis + improve withdrawal symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. NO INDICATION for bicarbonate in standard alcohol withdrawal. Reserved only for severe metabolic acidosis with hemodynamic compromise or pH <6.9. Standard treatment = benzodiazepines + thiamine + electrolyte replacement + hydration + supportive care.

opts.text: Administer GLUCOSE 50% IV bolus immediately to provide energy + correct hypoglycemia + improve mental status — patient appears mentally slow + needs rapid intervention
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. GLUCOSE WITHOUT THIAMINE FIRST can precipitate WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY. THIAMINE 100 mg IV BEFORE any glucose. Verify hypoglycemia first + treat thiamine first + then D50 if needed. Standard practice.

opts.text: Apply 4-POINT PHYSICAL RESTRAINTS immediately during alcohol withdrawal to prevent harm + control agitation + keep patient safe
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. RESTRAINTS WORSEN agitation + cause harm + violate dignity. STANDARD = 1:1 sitter for severe withdrawal/agitation + safe environment + bed rail padded + appropriate benzodiazepine therapy + multidisciplinary support. Restraints reserved for truly emergent situations + last resort + time-limited.

opts.text: Administer FLUMAZENIL (Romazicon) IV to reverse benzodiazepine effects if patient becomes oversedated from withdrawal protocol benzodiazepines
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. FLUMAZENIL CAN PRECIPITATE SEIZURES + WORSEN WITHDRAWAL in benzodiazepine-dependent patients (chronic alcohol use → chronic GABA modulation → flumazenil withdrawal phenomenon). STANDARD = if oversedation = reduce dose + monitor + supportive care; flumazenil RESERVED ONLY for life-threatening respiratory depression with EXTREME caution + readiness to manage withdrawal seizures.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at this alcohol withdrawal admission
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Alcohol Withdrawal Severity + DT Prevention + Benzodiazepine Management r/t CIWA-Ar 12 → 21 → 8 → 4 + withdrawal seizure + risk factors (prior DTs + chronic heavy use + age >40); SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED + SCHEDULED LORAZEPAM PROTOCOL with q1h CIWA-Ar; ICU transfer for severe withdrawal + escalation to phenobarbital/ketamine if refractory + continuous monitoring + 1:1 sitter + AVOID restraints when possible
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Alcohol withdrawal is time-sensitive + can progress rapidly to DTs with mortality. CIWA-Ar + benzodiazepine therapy + escalation foundation for safe management. RN role: continuous assessment + medication + monitoring.

opts.text: Wernicke-Korsakoff Prevention r/t THIAMINE 100 mg IV BEFORE any glucose-containing fluid (CRITICAL) + folate + multivitamin + magnesium + B12 + nutrition support; CRITICAL principle in chronic alcohol users; outpatient continuation thiamine + multivitamin + nutrition
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Wernicke-Korsakoff prevention is critical + time-sensitive + can cause permanent neurologic injury if missed. THIAMINE BEFORE GLUCOSE is fundamental principle. RN role: medication administration + family education.

opts.text: Seizure + Safety Surveillance + Multidisciplinary Coordination r/t withdrawal seizure × 1 at 18 hr; seizure precautions + bed rail padded + suction + airway management + 1:1 sitter; ICU transfer + continuous monitoring + addiction medicine + chaplain + social worker; cardiac + respiratory + neurological surveillance
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Seizure + safety surveillance during withdrawal critical. Multidisciplinary coordination essential. RN role: monitoring + safety + escalation + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: MAT + Outpatient Treatment + Lifelong AUD Management r/t addiction medicine consultation + NALTREXONE IM DEPOT (Vivitrol) 380 mg q month + 12-step program + AA + sober living + family therapy + couples therapy + community resources + insurance coordination + HCV outpatient hepatology + PCP medical home + chronic disease perspective
opts.rat: #4 — Significant + ongoing. MAT + outpatient + lifelong AUD management foundation for sustained recovery. Standard practice. RN role: education + coordination + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: Family-Centered + Emotional + Spiritual Support r/t wife (RN at outside hospital) + 2 teen daughters + brother + supportive but exhausted family; chaplain (Catholic) + social worker + family therapy + couples therapy + cultural sensitivity + transparent + compassionate communication + emotional support throughout
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + at discharge. Family-centered + emotional + spiritual support foundation for healing + sustained recovery. RN role: facilitate + advocate + emotional support.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient.

opts.text: THIAMINE 100 mg IV BEFORE any glucose-containing fluid (CRITICAL — prevents Wernicke encephalopathy); 100 mg IV daily × 5 days then PO 100 mg daily long-term; FOLATE 1 mg PO daily + MULTIVITAMIN PO daily + MAGNESIUM 2-4 g IV (low; replace empirically common in chronic alcohol use) + VITAMIN B12 1000 mcg IM/PO
opts.rat: Standard Wernicke-Korsakoff prevention in chronic alcohol use. CRITICAL principle. Standard practice. RN role: medication administration + family education.

opts.text: CIWA-Ar SCORE q1h initially + q2-4h when stable + symptom-triggered LORAZEPAM 2-4 mg IV q1h PRN for CIWA-Ar ≥10; escalation to 4 mg IV q30 min for CIWA ≥20; continuous infusion (midazolam 1-10 mg/hr) for refractory severe withdrawal; PHENOBARBITAL or KETAMINE adjunct for refractory; continuous cardiac monitoring + pulse oximetry + 1:1 sitter for severe withdrawal/agitation + bed rail padded + fall precautions + AVOID restraints when possible
opts.rat: Standard ASAM-aligned alcohol withdrawal management. CIWA-Ar symptom-triggered benzodiazepine protocol. Foundation for safe management. Standard practice.

opts.text: SEIZURE PRECAUTIONS + management — bed in low position + padded rails + suction at bedside + airway management ready + reduce stimuli + 1:1 sitter for high-risk; respond with airway + lorazepam 4 mg IV during/after seizure + postictal monitoring + neuro assessment + ICU transfer + continuous monitoring
opts.rat: Standard seizure precautions + response. Standard practice. RN role: recognition + safety + medication + escalation + family communication.

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. NO INDICATION for bicarbonate in standard alcohol withdrawal. Reserved only for severe metabolic acidosis with hemodynamic compromise or pH <6.9. Standard = benzodiazepines + thiamine + electrolyte replacement + hydration + supportive care.

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. GLUCOSE WITHOUT THIAMINE FIRST can precipitate WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY. THIAMINE 100 mg IV BEFORE any glucose. Verify hypoglycemia first + treat thiamine first + then D50 if needed.

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. RESTRAINTS WORSEN agitation + cause harm + violate dignity. STANDARD = 1:1 sitter + safe environment + bed rail padded + appropriate benzodiazepine therapy + multidisciplinary support. Restraints reserved for truly emergent situations + last resort + time-limited.

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. FLUMAZENIL CAN PRECIPITATE SEIZURES + WORSEN WITHDRAWAL in benzodiazepine-dependent patients. opts.text: Continue chronic outpatient benzodiazepine prescription after discharge (lorazepam 1 mg PO TID PRN anxiety) — patient has anxiety + sleep issues + benzodiazepine treats both effectively long-term
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. AVOID OUTPATIENT BENZODIAZEPINE prescription beyond acute withdrawal taper — dependence + accumulation + cross-tolerance with alcohol + relapse risk + iatrogenic substance use disorder. Standard: complete withdrawal taper + discontinue benzodiazepines + transition to non-benzodiazepine maintenance (gabapentin for anxiety + sleep; SSRI/SNRI for depression/anxiety; CBT; non-pharmacologic). MAT for AUD (Naltrexone) + outpatient + AA + community + relapse prevention.

opts.text: MAT OUTPATIENT PLANNING — NALTREXONE IM DEPOT (Vivitrol) 380 mg q month chosen for adherence + chronic disease + history of relapse + insurance coverage; first injection scheduled at addiction clinic 1-week post-discharge; alternatives = oral naltrexone + acamprosate + disulfiram + gabapentin + topiramate; AA + 12-step + sober living + community + relapse prevention + lifelong AUD management
opts.rat: Standard MAT + outpatient planning. Foundation for lifelong AUD management. RN role: education + coordination + multidisciplinary.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at this alcohol withdrawal admission
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Administer THIAMINE 100 mg IV BEFORE any glucose-containing fluid + magnesium 2 g IV + folate 1 mg PO + multivitamin PO + B12 1000 mcg IM (CRITICAL principle — prevents Wernicke encephalopathy + Korsakoff syndrome); reassess CIWA-Ar + initiate symptom-triggered lorazepam 2-4 mg IV q1h PRN for CIWA ≥10; continuous cardiac monitoring + pulse oximetry + bed alarm + fall precautions
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Thiamine BEFORE glucose is critical. Withdrawal medication protocol initiation. Foundation for safe management. RN role: rapid + protocol adherence.

opts.text: ASSESS CIWA-Ar q1h initially + monitor for escalation; respond to escalating CIWA with appropriate benzodiazepine dose escalation (2 mg → 4 mg IV; q1h → q30 min); ICU transfer for severe withdrawal (CIWA ≥20) + 1:1 sitter + continuous monitoring + AVOID restraints when possible; address electrolyte abnormalities + hydration
opts.rat: #2 — Second. CIWA-Ar assessment + escalation. Standard symptom-triggered protocol. RN role: continuous assessment + medication + escalation.

opts.text: Respond to WITHDRAWAL SEIZURE — protect airway + suction + bed rail padded + LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV during/after seizure + postictal monitoring + neuro assessment + ICU transfer; SEIZURE PRECAUTIONS continued throughout admission + continuous monitoring
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Seizure recognition + immediate response + safety + escalation. Standard practice. RN role: safety + medication + escalation.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1600 (15 min post initial treatment): patient feeling 'a bit better' + tremor slightly improved + CIWA-Ar reassessing; LORAZEPAM 2 mg IV given + thiamine 100 mg IV before any glucose + magnesium 2 g IV + folate + multivitamin + B12 + IV NS hydration; continuous monitoring + bed alarm + safety; family at bedside
opts.rat: Standard expected initial response. Foundation for ongoing recovery. RN role: continued monitoring + medication + reassessment.

opts.text: 2000 (4.5 hr post initial treatment; 14 hr post last drink — Stage 2): worsening to CIWA-Ar 21 (SEVERE; DT risk) with increasing tremor + diaphoresis + N/V + auditory hallucinations + agitation; LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV escalated; addiction medicine ICU consultation + transfer evaluation
opts.rat: Standard expected progression to severe withdrawal at 14 hr post last drink. RECOGNIZED + escalation appropriate. RN role: recognition + medication escalation + ICU activation + family communication.

opts.text: Day 2 0145 (18 hr post last drink): withdrawal seizure × 90 sec generalized tonic-clonic; LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV given; seizure precautions; postictal recovering + drowsy; ICU TRANSFER activated; 1:1 sitter + continuous monitoring + escalated benzo protocol
opts.rat: Standard expected withdrawal seizure at 18 hr (Stage 3 timing 12-48 hr). RECOGNIZED + immediate response + ICU transfer. RN role: safety + medication + escalation + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: Day 2 1000 (32 hr post last drink — DT risk peak managed): CIWA-Ar 8 (improving from 21 → 8); aggressive symptom-triggered + scheduled lorazepam over past 8 hr (total ~36 mg); tremor decreasing + appetite emerging + tolerating clear liquids → soft diet; addiction medicine completed + recommendations finalized + outpatient MAT planned
opts.rat: Standard expected response to aggressive benzodiazepine therapy + DT prevention. Foundation for ongoing recovery + multidisciplinary engagement.

opts.text: Day 3 morning 0700 (52 hr post last drink — DT peak passed; recovering): CIWA-Ar 4 (mild; below threshold); benzodiazepine taper proceeding (lorazepam 2 mg q4h PRN with target downtaper to discontinuation by Day 7); tremor minimal + no diaphoresis + no hallucinations + appetite + sleeping + improving; NALTREXONE IM DEPOT (Vivitrol) 380 mg planned for first appointment at addiction clinic 1-week post-discharge
opts.rat: Standard expected Day 3 recovery + MAT planning + benzodiazepine taper. Foundation for safe discharge + lifelong AUD management.

opts.text: Day 5 alternative scenario — RN attempted to administer GLUCOSE D50 IV without first giving thiamine to patient with apparent hypoglycemia (glucose 60); patient developed acute WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY (ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + encephalopathy) within hours; emergency thiamine 500 mg IV TID; permanent KORSAKOFF SYNDROME risk; sentinel event + RCA + just culture + system improvement
opts.rat: ⚠️ INAPPROPRIATE management (glucose without thiamine first in chronic alcohol user) → SEVERE Wernicke encephalopathy. This scenario demonstrates WHY thiamine BEFORE glucose is critical. RCA + just culture + system improvement + protocol education.

opts.text: Day 7 — DISCHARGE home with comprehensive plan: outpatient addiction medicine within 1 week (Vivitrol injection at appointment) + outpatient hepatology for HCV DAA treatment within 2 weeks + PCP medical home within 2 weeks + AA meetings commitment + sponsor relationship + family therapy + couples therapy + financial counseling + return-to-work + community resources + crisis resources + medical alert + lifelong AUD management commitment
opts.rat: Standard expected discharge + comprehensive plan + lifelong AUD partnership + multidisciplinary outpatient. Foundation for sustained recovery + family healing.

opts.text: Month 1 outpatient: NALTREXONE IM Vivitrol administered + AA meetings 4×/week + sponsor relationship + family therapy + couples therapy + financial counseling + return-to-work part-time + HCV DAA treatment started (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir 12-week course) + abstinent × 30 days + family supportive + chaplain ongoing + community engaged; outpatient addiction medicine engaged
opts.rat: Standard 1-month recovery trajectory + multidisciplinary engagement + MAT + AA + family + community. Foundation for sustained recovery.

opts.text: Month 6 — patient relapsed at family wedding under social pressure × 1 day binge drinking; called addiction medicine + AA sponsor + family support + immediate recommitment + extended Vivitrol injection + continued AA + family therapy + relapse not equal to failure (~5-7 attempts typical before sustained sobriety); ongoing engagement + outpatient + community
opts.rat: Standard relapse pattern + appropriate response (recommit + extend MAT + community support). Relapse is common in AUD (~5-7 attempts typical before sustained sobriety). RN role: non-judgmental + reframe + recommit + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: 1-year follow-up: sustained sobriety × 11 months (1 month relapse at month 6 resolved); HCV cured with DAA treatment + abstinence; Vivitrol injection × 12 months + Naltrexone IM ongoing; AA meetings 3×/week + sponsor + advocacy + service work; family thriving + wife + daughters supportive + extended family involved; employed full-time + financial recovery; quality of life excellent; lifelong AUD management; recovery + community + service + meaning-making
